topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. "Effective Spring Semester, the Department of Literature will be ceasing operations." I reread the subject line three times, my thumb hovering over the screen, unable to process the sterile language. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic of my desk vibrating slightly under my grip. I stared blankly at the framed photo of the campus quad, the sunlight in the picture feeling suddenly mocking. A hollow ache settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the sudden silence of my small dorm room. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the sprawling campus, now looking less like a vibrant hub of learning and more like a skeleton waiting for the wind to dismantle it.

[story 2]

"No, no, this can't be right." I muttered the words aloud, the sound catching in my throat like grit. I kept running my fingers over the worn spine of my favorite philosophy textbook, seeking some anchor in the familiar. Each page felt flimsy, irrelevant now. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I traced the faded inscription on the cover. The air in the hallway seemed thick, heavy with unspoken finality. I paced the narrow confines of my apartment, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the bare floorboards. I stopped abruptly in front of my closet, pulling out old notebooks filled with frantic, half-formed ideas. They seemed ridiculous now—a futile attempt to preserve something that was already dissolving. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to scrub the news away, but the image of the closing announcement refused to fade.

[story 3]

The administrative office felt cold, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, high-pitched whine. When the dean finally delivered the news, the words seemed to swim before my eyes. I could feel a tightness constricting around my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath. I nodded, a jerky, mechanical motion, trying to assemble a response that felt utterly inadequate. "So, what does that mean for us?" My voice came out thin, reedy, lacking any real resonance. I looked down at my worn sneakers, suddenly intensely focused on the scuff marks on the rubber. The world outside the window seemed muted, as if someone had turned down the saturation on reality. I stood there for a long moment, rooted to the spot, simply absorbing the sheer emptiness of the announcement, a profound sense of disorientation washing over me.
